---
title: "Topic Template"
description: "Topic description (max characters 175)"
author:
  - name: Author One
  - name: Author Two
date: "04/18/2026"
date-modified: now
draft: true
image: "assets/img/thumbnail.png"
categories:
  - Topic
# other-links:     
#   - text: Template Download
#     href: assets/project-template.zip
#     icon: file-zip
# resources:
#  - assets/project-template.zip
---

## Purpose of this page

Use this template to create a **topic page** for the course website.

This page must work in two navigation views:

- **By Topic (Concept-first):** students explore concepts directly
- **By Learning Path (Sequence-first):** students follow lecture units in order

Your topic page should therefore explain the concept clearly **and** show where it fits in the course sequence.

## Draft mode (important)

As long as the document option `draft: true` is set:

- draft posts appear when you preview the site
- draft posts are blank when you render the site
- draft posts do not appear in listings, navigation, the site map, or site search

You can read more in **Website Drafts**.

To publish the post when it is complete, remove `draft: true` from the document options and then render the site.

## Author checklist (before writing)

Fill these items first:

- topic title and short description
- authors
- topic thumbnail image
- topic category tags
- related learning path units (for cross-links)
- prerequisites and estimated study time

## 1) Topic at a glance

### Learning outcomes

After completing this topic, students can:

- [Outcome 1]
- [Outcome 2]
- [Outcome 3]

### Prerequisites

- [Required prior knowledge]
- [Optional prior knowledge]

### Estimated effort

- Self-study: [e.g., 45–60 minutes]
- Practice: [e.g., 30 minutes]

## 2) Core concept (Concept-first view)

### Why this topic matters

[Write 1 short paragraph about relevance for Informatics / Computer Science and Information Systems students.]

### Key ideas

1. [Key idea 1]
2. [Key idea 2]
3. [Key idea 3]

### Typical mistakes and misconceptions

- [Misconception 1]
- [Misconception 2]

## 3) Worked examples

### Example A (basic)

- Context: [Short scenario]
- Problem: [What must be solved]
- Solution steps: [Step-by-step explanation]
- Takeaway: [What students should remember]

### Example B (applied)

- Context: [CS or IS real-world context]
- Problem: [What must be solved]
- Solution steps: [Step-by-step explanation]
- Takeaway: [What students should remember]

## 4) Practice and self-check

### Practice tasks

1. [Task 1]
2. [Task 2]
3. [Task 3]

### Self-check questions

- [Question 1]
- [Question 2]
- [Question 3]

## 6) Teaching notes (for instructors)

### Suggested in-class use

- [How to introduce]
- [Where to pause for discussion]
- [What to emphasize]

### Adaptation options

- For Computer Science focus: [adaptation]
- For Information Systems focus: [adaptation]

## 7) References and resources

- [Book / article / open educational resource]
- [Tool or dataset]
- [Optional advanced reading]

## Quality gate (before publishing)

- Learning outcomes are measurable and clear
- At least one worked example is included
- Practice tasks and self-check questions are present
- Cross-links to related learning-path units are included
- Language is concise and undergraduate-friendly
